result:
```python
import re

def validate_signup(email: str, password: str, username: str) -> list[str]:
    errors = []

    if not re.match(r'^[^@\s]+@[^@\s]+\.[^@\s]+$', email):
        errors.append("invalid email format")

    if len(password) < 8:
        errors.append("password must be at least 8 characters")

    if not any(c.isdigit() for c in password):
        errors.append("password must contain a digit")

    if not re.match(r'^[a-zA-Z0-9]{3,20}$', username):
        errors.append("username must be alphanumeric and 3-20 characters")

    return errors
```

Returns empty list on success. Example:

```python
validate_signup("bad-email", "short1", "ab")
# ["invalid email format", "password must be at least 8 characters", "username must be alphanumeric and 3-20 characters"]

validate_signup("user@example.com", "securepass9", "alice123")
# []
```

Password splits into two checks so callers know exactly which constraint failed. Email regex is intentionally simple — rejects obvious invalids without false negatives on valid edge cases.
